transcript: Welcome to Lesson 4.3, Designing Effective CTAs or Call to Actions. In Lesson 4.1, you learned the 7 anatomy elements of a high-converting landing page. In Lesson 4.2, you learned how to write headlines and copy that build desire and trust. Now we arrive at the moment everything has been building toward, the CTA. The single element that determines whether all that work pays off or not. Your CTA is the most clicked element on your page. It is the doorway between a visitor and a lead. Get it right and conversion climb. Get it wrong and even the best headline and copy cannot save the page. By the end of this lesson, you will know exactly what makes a CTA irresistible, where to place it, how to design it for maximum visibility, and the most common mistakes that silently destroy conversion rates. Let's dive in. Before we get into the tactical elements, you need to understand the psychology behind clicking. Because clicking is not a mechanical action, it is a decision. And every decision has friction and motivation on either side of it. On the friction side, people do not click when the outcome is unclear. If a visitor is not sure what happens after they click the button, their instinct is to not click at all. They do not click when the value does not feel worth the exchange. They do not click when they feel like they are committing to something they are not ready for. They do not click when the button does not stand out visually. And they do not click when there is no reason to act right now versus waiting. On the motivation side, people click when they know exactly what they will receive and it feels valuable. They click when the exchange feels low risk or free. They click when the button is impossible to miss. They click when the page has built perfectly to that moment. When all the copy has done its job. And they click when there is a reason to act now rather than later. Our entire CTA design strategy is about maximizing motivation and minimizing friction at the same moment. Everything from the words on the button to the space around it to the single line of text below it is working together to make clicking feel like the obvious, easy, and rewarding thing to do. Every high-performing CTA has five distinct elements, and each one has a specific job. Think of these as the engineering aspects of your button. Miss one and your conversion rate beats the price. Element number one is text. The words on the button are the last piece of copy a visitor reads before deciding. They must describe what the visitor receives, not what they have to do. Element number two is size. Your button must be physically large enough to see and click without effort, both on a laptop and on a mobile phone screen. Element number three is placement. Where the button appears on the page determines which types of visitors you capture. Strategic placement covers multiple decision moments. Element number four is contrast. The button must be visually distinct from everything around it. It must be the element that the eye naturally lands on in the section of the page. Element number five is microcopy. This is the short line of text directly below the button that removes the single biggest hesitation your persona has at the moment of clicking. Let me go deep on each of these so you can understand not just what they are but exactly how to execute them. CTA text is the highest leveraged word on your entire page. Despite Despite being the shortest copy element it carries enormous weight because it is the last thing a visitor reads before they decide The core rule is this Describe what they receive not what they do When you write submit you are describing a mechanical action. There is no emotional reward in submitting something. When you write yes, send me the guide, you are describing the reward. There is value in receiving a guide. That difference in perception changes behavior. Here is a transformation table to make this concrete. Submit becomes yes, send me the guide. Download becomes get my free checklist now. Sign up becomes create my free account. Register becomes save my spot. Click here becomes show me how it works. Send becomes start getting more leads. Every weak version describes an action. Every strong version describes a reward. Now here's the principle that many experienced marketers overlook. First person language. Writing the button text from the visitor's point of view using words like I, me, and my can increase conversion rates by up to 90% according to multiple split tests. The difference between get your free guide and get my free guide is one word, but that one word shifts the psychological ownership. The visitor reads my and feels like the guide already belongs to them. The click becomes a claim, not a submission. This is a small change with a significant impact. Make it a habit on every button you write. Elements 2 and 4 are both about visibility, and they work together. A correctly sized button that blends into the background is still invisible. A high-contrast button that is too small to tap on mobile is still a friction point. You need both. For size, follow these rules. The minimum button height is 44 pixels, which is Apple's recommended touch target for mobile interfaces. anything smaller becomes difficult to tap accurately on a phone screen the width should be at least 200 pixels though on mobile the best practice is to make the button full width so a thumb can tap it anywhere across the screen the font size inside the button should be 16 to 20 pixels minimum small text inside a button looks unconfident and gets missed here is the simplisize principle. When in doubt, make it bigger. Nobody has ever complained that a CTA button was too easy to find. Err on the side of prominent. For contrast, the rule is that your button must visually stand out from everything around it. It should be the most visually dominant element in its section of the page. Here's an important nuance. Contrast is not only about color. You can create contrast through shape, size, and white space as well. Surrounding your button with empty space creates visual isolation that naturally draws the eye. A button buried inside a block of text with no breathing room around it disappears into the noise, even if its color is technically different from the background. A practical test, look at your landing page and unfocus your eyes slightly. Whatever element your blurry gaze lands on first is the most visually dominant. It should be your CTA button. If it's not, adjust the size, spacing, or contrast until it is. Placement is about understanding that different visitors reach their decision moment at different points on the page. A single CTA position serves only one type of visitor. Strategic placement serves all of them. There are three positions every landing page should consider. Position number one is above the fold. This is non-negotiable. It must always be there. Some visitors arrive at your page already warm Maybe they saw multiple pieces of your content before clicking the ad or they were referred by a trusted colleague These visitors are ready to act immediately and should not have to scroll to find the button A CTA above the fold serves them immediately. Position number two is mid-page, placed immediately after your benefit bullets. Some visitors need to read why your offer is valuable before they are convinced. Once they finish the bullets and feel the desire, they want to act. If the next CTA is still far below at the bottom of the page, they may lose momentum while scrolling. A mid-page button captures medium intent visitor at their peak readiness. Position number three is the bottom of the page. This position is always repeated, even if you already have two buttons above it. The visitor who scrolls all the way to the bottom of a landing page is highly engaged. They read everything, the headline, the benefits, the social proof, trust indicators, and they are now making their final decision. Reward that engagement with a clear, easy button right where their attention is. For short lead capture pages, you typically need positions 1 and 3. For longer sales pages, use all three positions. You are not overwhelming the visitor with options. You are simply making sure every type of visitor encounters the button exactly when they are ready. Element 5 is microcopy. And despite being just one line of text, it is often the difference between a visitor clicking and a visitor hesitating. Microcopy is the short line placed directly below your CTE button that addresses the single biggest fear your persona has at the moment of clicking. Think about what is going through your visitor's mind right before they click. Are they worried about spam? Write no spam, unsubscribe anytime. Are they worried about cost? Write 100% free, no credit card ever needed. Are they worried about commitment? Write cancel anytime in one click. Are they worried about time? Write takes less than 60 seconds to get started. Is there scarcity that creates soft urgency? Write only eight spots remaining this month. The rule is one line only. Do not write a paragraph of reassurance. Visitors at the bottom are making a split-second decision. A single, clear, trust-building line is read and believed. A paragraph is ignored. Identify the one primary fear your specific persona has and address that one fear. Now let me quickly cover the three CTA types so you know which to use in different situations. A button CTA is a standalone clickable button. This is your primary conversion tool on landing pages and the type you will build most often in GoHighLevel. An inline text CTA is a hyperlink phrase within body copy. This works well in emails and blog posts as a secondary action. But it should never be your primary conversion element on a landing page because it is too easy to miss. A form submit button is the button at the bottom of an opt-in form. Technically, a button CTA, but specifically attached to a form. On lead capture pages in Go High Level, your primary CTA is most often a form submit button with the reward language applied directly to it. Let me walk you through the 7 most common CTA mistakes I see on landing pages so you can audit every page you build before publishing. Mistake number 1. Using submit, download, or click here as button text. These words describe actions, not rewards. Replace with reward language every single time. Mistake number two a button that blends into the page background If you have to squint to find the button it does not have enough contrast Adjust the visual weight until it stands out clearly Mistake number three The CTA only appears at the bottom of a long page Visitors who are ready to act immediately must not have to scroll through your entire page just to find the button. Always place one above the fold. Mistake number four, two or more competing CTAs on the same page with different destinations. When you give visitors two choices, you split their attention and reduce the conversion on both. Every landing page has one goal, one CTA, one destination. Mistake number five, a button too small to click comfortably on mobile. This one is extraordinarily common. Always preview your page on a mobile device before publishing. If your thumb cannot tap the button without accidentally hitting something else, make it larger. Mistake number six. No microcopy below the button. You have done all the work of building desire and trust through your headline, copy, and social proof. Do not abandon the visitor at the final moment. One line below the button addresses their last hesitation and closes the conversion. Mistake number seven. The CTA is surrounded by too much competing content. Copy, images, and other elements pressed up against the button visually dilute the prominence. Wrap white space around your button. Isolation draws the eye. A button with breathing room gets clicked far more often than a button buried in a busy layout. Fixing even two to three of these mistakes on a page you have already built can double your conversion rate without changing anything else. Let's recap what you learned in this lesson. Clicking is a decision, and your CTA must make that decision feel easy, low-risk, and rewarding. Every design element, from the words to the white space, either adds friction or removes it. The five elements of a high-performing CTA are text, size, placement, contrast, and microcopy. Each has a specific job, and all five must work together. CTA text should describe what the visitor receives, not what they do. First-person language using words like my and me increases conversion by up to 90% compared to generic button text. CTAs must be placed strategically in three positions. Above the fold, always, mid-page on longer pages after the benefits section, and repeated at the bottom for visitors who need all the evidence first. Microcopy is one single line below the button that addresses the biggest fear your persona has at the moment of clicking. One line, not a paragraph. Address the one fear and remove it. And the three CTA types are button, inline text, and form submit. On landing pages, always use button or form submit as your primary conversion action. Never rely on inline text alone. Coming up next is lesson 4.4, using images, videos, and social proof. You have now mastered the anatomy, copy, and CTE design of a high-converting landing page. The next lesson teaches you how to use visual elements and social proof to make every page undeniably credible and emotionally compelling. You will learn how to choose the right images, how to use video effectively, and the exact placement rules for testimonials and proof elements that maximize their conversion impact. Visual elements and social proof together can increased landing page conversions by 30 to 80 percent. This lesson shows you exactly how to use them. Let's continue to lesson 4.4.

visualDescriptions.description:
The image presents a screenshot of an online lesson on designing effective CTAs (Call-to-Actions), with a clear and concise layout. The title, "Designing Effective CTAs," is prominently displayed at the top, accompanied by a brief description that reads, "The most clicked element on your page - learn to design it right."

**Key Information:**

* **Module and Lesson:** The lesson is part of Module 4, Lesson 4.3.
* **Estimated Time:** The estimated time to complete the lesson is 14 minutes.
* **What You'll Learn:** A list of six bullet points outlines the key takeaways from the lesson:
	+ The psychology behind why people click a CTA - or scroll past it
	+ The 5 elements that make every CTA high-performing
	+ Where to place CTAs on a landing page for maximum conversions
	+ CTA design principles that draw the eye and demand attention
	+ The 3 CTA types and when to use each one
	+ The most common CTA mistakes and exactly how to fix them

**Visual Elements:**

* A green button with white text reading "GET MY FREE GUIDE" is displayed on the right side of the image, accompanied by a small icon of a target and a graph showing an upward trend.
* A logo for "Libre" is located in the top-right corner.

**Background and Design:**

* The background of the image is white, with a green border at the top.
* The overall design is clean and modern, making it easy to read and understand the information presented.

The image presents a slide titled "The 5 Elements of Every High-Performing CTA" with the subtitle "Miss one and your conversion rate pays the price - engineer your CTA for success." The title is displayed in bold black text at the top, accompanied by a green line underneath.

Below the title, a diagram illustrates the five essential elements of a high-performing Call-to-Action (CTA), each represented by a blue circle with a white number. The elements are:

*   **Element 1: Text**: Action language describing reward
*   **Element 2: Size**: Large enough to click easily
*   **Element 3: Placement**: Visible at decision moments
*   **Element 4: Contrast**: Stands out from background
*   **Element 5: No Credit Card**: Removes final hesitation

A green button in the center of the diagram reads "Get My Free Guide" and features the text "No credit card required. Instant access."

Below the diagram, a table outlines the rules for each element:

| **Element** | **The Rule** |
| :---------- | :----------- |
| **Text**    | Describe the reward (what they get), not the action (what they do). |
| **Size**    | Minimum: 44 pixels tall. Ideally spans 50% of content width on desktop, 100% on mobile. |
| **Placement** | Always above the fold. Repeat mid-page on long pages. Always at the very bottom. |
| **Contrast** | Button must be the most visually dominant element. Use a color that pops against the background. |

In the top-right corner, the word "Libre" appears in green text. The background of the slide is light blue.

The image presents a slide from a presentation focused on crafting effective Call-to-Action (CTA) text. The title, **"CTA Text: The Words That Get the Click,"** is prominently displayed at the top.

**Key Points:**

* **The Golden Rule:** A crucial principle for creating effective CTA text is highlighted: "Describe what they RECEIVE — not what they DO." This emphasizes the importance of focusing on the benefits or rewards that users will receive, rather than the actions they need to take.
* **Weak (Action-Focused) vs. Strong (Reward-Focused) CTAs:** The slide compares two types of CTA text:
	+ **Weak (Action-Focused) CTAs:**
		- Submit
		- Download
		- Sign Up
		- Register
		- Click Here
		- Send
	+ **Strong (Reward-Focused) CTAs:**
		- Yes, Send Me the Guide
		- Get My Free Checklist Now
		- Create My Free Account
		- Save My Spot
		- Show Me How It Works
		- Start Getting More Leads

**Visuals:**

* The background of the slide features a white grid pattern.
* A green bar runs along the top and right side of the slide.
* The Libre logo is displayed in the top-right corner.

**Takeaway:**

The slide effectively illustrates the difference between weak, action-focused CTAs and strong, reward-focused CTAs. By emphasizing the benefits and rewards that users will receive, businesses can create more compelling and effective CTAs that drive engagement and conversions.

The image presents a slide from a presentation focused on creating effective call-to-action (CTA) buttons, emphasizing the importance of size and contrast. The title, "Size and Contrast: Make Your CTA Impossible to Miss," sets the tone for the content.

* **Element 2: SIZE**
	+ A visual representation of a button transitioning from too small to an optimal size
	+ Key points:
		- Min Height: 44px (Apple's recommended tap target)
		- Width: At least 20px ~ ideally 50% of content width
		- Font Size: 18 to 20px minimum for readability
		- Mobile: Full-width buttons for easy thumb tapping
	+ Guiding principle: "When in doubt, make it bigger."
* **Element 4: CONTRAST**
	+ A visual representation of buttons with varying levels of contrast
	+ Key points:
		- Button must visually stand out from background
		- Use White Space around button to amplify presence
		- Never let buttons blend into surrounding sections
		- Must be the most visually dominant element near the form
	+ Factors contributing to contrast: Color + Shape + Size + White Space
* **The 3-Second Test**
	+ A guiding principle for evaluating the effectiveness of a CTA: "Unfocus your eyes. If the CTA doesn't pop immediately, fix it."

In summary, the slide provides actionable tips for designing CTAs that are impossible to miss, focusing on optimal size and sufficient contrast to ensure they stand out and encourage user interaction.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to strategic Call-to-Action (CTA) placement on landing pages, emphasizing the importance of placement in driving conversions. The title, "CTA Placement: Put It Where Decisions Happen," sets the tone for the informative content that follows.

**Key Points:**

* **Placement is not guesswork**: The infographic highlights three strategic positions for CTAs on every landing page.
* **Three Strategic Positions:**
	+ **Above the Fold (Pos #1)**
		- When: Always (Non-negotiable)
		- Removes zero-effort barrier for high-intent visitors who are ready to act immediately
	+ **Mid-Page (Pos #2)**
		- When: Longer pages (After benefits)
		- Captures medium-intent visitors who are persuaded by the benefit bullets
	+ **Bottom of Page (Pos #3)**
		- When: Always (The Repeater)
		- Rewards highly engaged visitors who scroll through prior and trust indicators

**Visual Representation:**

* A mockup of a mobile phone screen displays a sample landing page with three CTAs, each corresponding to one of the strategic positions mentioned above.

**Additional Insights:**

* A black bar at the bottom of the image provides additional guidance on CTA placement:
	+ Short Page: Top + Bottom Only
	+ Long Page: All 3 Positions

In summary, the infographic provides a clear and concise guide to effective CTA placement on landing pages, highlighting the importance of strategic positioning to maximize conversions. By understanding the different types of visitors and their behaviors, marketers can optimize their CTA placement to drive better results.

The image presents a slide from a presentation on "Micro-Copy and CTA Types" by Libre. The title is displayed in black text at the top, accompanied by a green bar.

**Element 5: Micro-Copy**

*   A blue button with white text reads "Get Instant Access," followed by smaller text stating "No credit card needed. 100% Free."
*   **The Conversion Insurance**
    *   One short line of text placed directly below the CTA button that removes the single biggest fear preventing the click.
    *   **Common Patterns**
        *   No spam
        *   No card needed
        *   Guarantee
        *   < 60 seconds
    *   **Design Choices**
        *   Rule: One line only. Address the ONE biggest fear.

**The 3 CTA Types**

*   **Button CTA**
    *   Standalone clickable button element.
    *   BEST FOR: Primary page action & main goal
    *   Example: "Buy, Send Me the System"
*   **Inline Text CTA**
    *   Hyperlinked text within body copy paragraphs.
    *   BEST FOR: Secondary actions (blogs, emails)
    *   Example: "Click here to download"
*   **Form Submit CTA**
    *   The button at the bottom of an opt-in form.
    *   BEST FOR: Lead capture with visible forms
    *   Example: "Get My Free Checklist"

A pro tip at the bottom of the slide advises using a Button CTA or Form Submit Button on landing pages, as relying solely on inline text for primary conversion actions is not recommended.

The image presents a slide from a presentation titled "7 CTA Mistakes That Kill Conversions," which aims to educate viewers on common errors to avoid when creating call-to-action (CTA) buttons. The slide is divided into two columns, with the left column listing the mistakes and the right column providing the corresponding fixes.

* **Mistake 1: Using "Submit," "Download," or "Click Here"**
	+ Description: Using generic CTAs that do not provide clear value to the user.
	+ Fix: Replace with reward language, such as "Get My Guide."
* **Mistake 2: Button blends into the page background**
	+ Description: CTAs that are not visually distinct from the surrounding content.
	+ Fix: Ensure strong contrast and visual dominance.
* **Mistake 3: CTA only appears at the bottom of the page**
	+ Description: Placing the CTA too far down the page, reducing its visibility.
	+ Fix: Add CTAs above the fold, mid-page, and bottom.
* **Mistake 4: Two or more competing CTAs on one page**
	+ Description: Having multiple CTAs that compete for the user's attention.
	+ Fix: Stick to one primary CTA per landing page.
* **Mistake 5: Button is too small on mobile screens**
	+ Description: CTAs that are not optimized for mobile devices.
	+ Fix: Min 44px tall & full-width for easy tapping.
* **Mistake 6: No micro-copy below the button**
	+ Description: Not providing additional context or reassurance below the CTA.
	+ Fix: Add one line addressing the primary fear.
* **Mistake 7: Surrounded by too much competing content**
	+ Description: CTAs that are overwhelmed by surrounding content.
	+ Fix: Add white space to visually isolate the button.

The slide concludes with a note stating that fixing just 2-3 of these mistakes on an existing page can double the conversion rate overnight. Overall, the slide provides actionable tips for improving the effectiveness of CTAs and increasing conversions.

The image presents a summary of key takeaways from Lesson 4.3, focusing on effective call-to-action (CTA) strategies. The main points are:

• **Clicking is a Decision**
	+ Emphasizes that CTAs must make the decision feel easy, low-risk, and rewarding by removing friction and communicating clear value.

• **The 5 Elements**
	+ Highlights that every high-performing CTA optimizes Text, Size, Placement, Contrast, and Micro-Copy — missing one can lead to conversion suffering.

• **First-Person Rewards**
	+ Suggests describing the reward ("Get MY Free Guide") instead of the effort ("Submit"), which can increase conversions by 90%.

• **Strategic Positioning**
	+ Recommends placing CTAs Above the Fold (always), Mid-Page (after benefits), and at the Bottom to capture ready, convinced, and cautious visitors.

• **Micro-Copy Insurance**
	+ Advises adding one short line directly below the button (e.g., "No credit card needed") to address the single biggest fear stopping the click.

• **Primary CTA Types**
	+ Suggests always using a high-contrast Button or Form Submit button as the primary action on landing pages — never relying on inline text links.

In summary, the image provides six key takeaways for creating effective CTAs, including making clicking a decision, optimizing the five essential elements, using first-person rewards, strategic positioning, micro-copy insurance, and primary CTA types. By implementing these strategies, businesses can improve their conversion rates and enhance user experience.
